tema: Used to — Past Habits That Are Gone

banco_oraciones.oracion: Abuela Carmen used to live in the countryside.
I used to play soccer every Sunday.
Ben used to have long hair — there are photos.
People used to write letters by hand.
She didn't use to like coffee. Now she drinks three cups a day.
I didn't use to speak English.
We didn't use to have internet at home.
Did you use to play with your cousins?
Where did she use to live?
Did people use to dance more?
Did you use to play soccer? Yes, I did.
Did Ben use to have long hair? Yes, he did.
Did she use to like coffee? No, she didn't.

interaccion: Abuela Carmen: I **used to live** in the countryside. We **used to swim** in the river and dance cumbia every Saturday.
Kenji: **Did you use to** have electricity?
Abuela Carmen: Electricity? We **used to have** stars, my dear.
Ben: I **used to have** long hair. — Sofía: And we **used to think** it was a good idea. We don't anymore.

banner_diagnostico.opcion_a: I use to play soccer when I was a kid.
banner_diagnostico.opcion_b: I used to play soccer when I was a kid.

inicio.definicion_pragmatica: 'Used to' describes habits and situations that were true in the past but are not true anymore — I used to..., but now I don't.

filas: She used to live in the countryside
I didn't use to like coffee
Did you use to play outside?
Yes, I did. / No, she didn't.
Did you use to...? Yes, I did.
past habit or state, no longer true
I used to..., but now I don't
Now: I usually walk · Past: I used to walk

ejercicios.pregunta: I ___ play soccer when I was a kid.
___ you use to play with your cousins?

ejercicios.afirmacion: In negatives and questions, 'used to' loses the -d: didn't use to, Did you use to...?

ejercicios.texto: Abuela Carmen ___ (live) in the countryside when she was young. (used to)
We ___ (not / have) internet at home. (use to)
Where did she ___ (live) before Medellín? (use to)

ejercicios.respuesta: Ben used to have long hair
Did you use to play outside

ejercicios.texto_con_error: I use to play soccer when I was a kid.
ejercicios.respuesta: I used to play soccer when I was a kid.

ejercicios.texto_con_error: She didn't used to like coffee.
ejercicios.respuesta: She didn't use to like coffee.

ejercicios.texto_con_error: Did they used to dance cumbia?
ejercicios.respuesta: Did they use to dance cumbia?
People didn't use to write letters.

ejercicios.oracion_base: She used to live in the countryside.
ejercicios.respuesta: Did she use to live in the countryside?

ejercicios.oracion_base: Did you use to play soccer?

ejercicios.respuesta: I didn't use to like reggaeton.

ejercicios.pregunta: Did you use to watch cartoons? (short answer)
Did you use to speak English as a baby? (short answer, No)

guion: Kenji: Abuela, what did you use to do on Saturdays?
Abuela Carmen: We used to go to the river in the morning and dance cumbia at night.
Sofía: Did you use to cook with your mother?
Abuela Carmen: Every day. We didn't use to buy bread — we used to make it.
Ben: I used to make bread too! Once. It was... technically bread.
Abuela Carmen: My dear, some people used to call that a brick.

comprension.pregunta: What did Abuela Carmen use to do on Saturday nights?
What did people use to call Ben's bread?

reading.texto: Medellín used to be a very different city. Abuela Carmen remembers: 'The metro didn't use to exist — we used to take old buses that broke down every week. People used to carry umbrellas every day, because you never knew. And on Sundays, the whole neighborhood used to eat together in the street.' Some things have changed: now there is a metro, and the buses are new. But some things haven't: 'We still eat together on Sundays,' she says. 'Some habits are too delicious to lose.'

preguntas.afirmacion: The metro used to exist when Abuela Carmen was young.
The old buses used to break down every week.
The neighborhood used to eat together on Sundays.
